---
page-layout: custom
---

```{=html}
<div class="hero-section">
  <div class="hero-content">
    <span class="hero-label">PIONEERING P53 THERAPEUTICS</span>
    <h1>The Future of Cancer Research is Here</h1>
    <h3>Advancing innovative tumor-specific therapies through revolutionary research to shatter the global cancer burden</h3>
    <blockquote class="mission-statement">
      <p>Current cancer research needs supercharging so that leaps in next-generation therapies become commonplace. At Institute 53, we dream bigger, we flip the script on cancer, and we definitely don't play by the rules. Institute 53 scientists are not content with the next step — they are going far beyond &ldquo;taking it up a notch.&rdquo;</p>
    </blockquote>
    <!--<div class="hero-buttons">
      <a href="research.html" class="btn btn-primary btn-lg">Explore Research</a>
      <a href="collaborate.html" class="btn btn-outline-light btn-lg">Join the Network</a>
    </div>-->
  </div>
  <div class="hero-image">
    <img src="images/stock-microscope.jpg" alt="Microscope">
  </div>
</div>

<div class="section-intro">
  <div class="section-intro-wrapper">
    <h2>Pioneering the Next Generation of p53 Cancer Therapeutics</h2>
    <div class="section-intro-image parallax-container">
      <img src="images/stock-cell.jpg" alt="Cell research" class="parallax-image">
    </div>
    <div class="section-intro-content">
      <h4 class="section-intro-subheader">A Case of Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde</h4>
      <p>The p53 gene and p53 protein have been a major focus in cancer research for more than 45 years, and much has been learned to leverage discoveries into clever strategies for killing cancer. This continues to remain a challenge that results from the remarkable ability of p53 to play the dichotomous role of Dr. Jekyll fighting against cancer and Mr. Hyde causing cancer. Throughout the years of scientists trying to target p53 therapeutically, the Mr. Hyde in p53 has stayed a step ahead, so at Institute 53, we have veered off in a completely new direction; to trick the Mr. Hyde of p53 to do the cancer killing for us. <button class="learn-more-btn" onclick="document.getElementById('science-modal').classList.add('active');document.body.style.overflow='hidden'">Learn More &rarr;</button></p>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- Science Modal -->
<div id="science-modal" class="science-modal-overlay" onclick="if(event.target===this){this.classList.remove('active');document.body.style.overflow=''}">  
  <div class="science-modal-content">
    <button class="science-modal-close" onclick="document.getElementById('science-modal').classList.remove('active');document.body.style.overflow=''">&times;</button>
    <span class="section-label">THE SCIENCE BEHIND INSTITUTE 53</span>
    <h2>The Science Supporting Institute 53</h2>
    <p>p53 was first discovered as an oncogene and oncoprotein when mutated, and later as a tumor suppressor in its wildtype form, earning it the moniker <em>Guardian of the Genome</em>. This dichotomy — suppressing cancer and promoting cancer — is unique in the field of cancer research.</p>
    <p>There are two general types of p53 mutations that cause cancer: those that merely knockout the p53 gene (such as a deletion), and missense mutations predominantly within p53's DNA Binding Domain that not only eliminate p53's tumor suppressor function but also unleash p53's cancer-promoting properties. These missense mutations are referred to as Gain-Of-Function (GOF), in which new transcriptional activation of genes results — many of which drive cancer development, metastasis, and drug resistance.</p>
    <p>It has long been intriguing how so many different missense mutations in p53 could generate the same type of novel gene activation properties. GOF mutated p53 exhibits promoter binding for activated genes not through direct DNA binding as wildtype p53 does, but through interaction with transcription factors such as Sp1 and ETS1. At least two factors define promoters that bind GOF mutated p53: multiplicity of transcription factor binding sites and appropriate spacing of these sites.</p>
    <p>With this mechanistic insight, investigators at Institute 53 have developed therapeutically beneficial genes driven by GOF mutated p53 to create cancer-killing agents. A new era of gene therapy for cancer treatment is now on the horizon.</p>
    <h4 class="section-intro-subheader" style="margin-top:2rem;">Cancers in Our Sights</h4>
    <ul class="science-cancer-list">
      <li>Lung Cancer <span>(including the deadly Small Cell Lung Cancer)</span></li>
      <li>Ovarian Cancer</li>
      <li>Pancreatic Cancer</li>
      <li>Esophageal Cancer</li>
      <li>Head and Neck Cancer</li>
    </ul>
    <h4 class="section-intro-subheader" style="margin-top:2rem;">A Word from Our Founder</h4>
    <p><strong>Dr. Sumitra Deb</strong> is founder and director of Institute 53 — an institute that is the culmination of p53 investigation over the past 40 years. Much of the discovery of p53's cancer-causing properties has been pioneered by Dr. Deb. With a distinguished record of p53 research and over 30 years of deciphering how p53 mutations contribute to oncogenesis, he has played a leading role in the biochemical understanding of oncogenic phenotypes of mutant p53. Dr. Deb has served as editor of three books, co-chaired NIH study sections, chaired a cancer-research funding committee, and assembled an exceptional team of collaborators to drive Institute 53's mission forward.</p>
  </div>
</div>

<script>
document.addEventListener('keydown', function(e) {
  if (e.key === 'Escape') {
    document.getElementById('science-modal').classList.remove('active');
    document.body.style.overflow = '';
  }
});

document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', function() {
  const parallaxImage = document.querySelector('.section-intro .parallax-image');
  const container = document.querySelector('.section-intro');
  
  window.addEventListener('scroll', function() {
    const rect = container.getBoundingClientRect();
    const scrolled = window.scrollY;
    const containerTop = container.offsetTop;
    const speed = 0.3;
    
    if (rect.top < window.innerHeight && rect.bottom > 0) {
      const yPos = (scrolled - containerTop) * speed;
      parallaxImage.style.transform = 'translateY(' + yPos + 'px)';
    }
  });
});
</script>
```

<!--
```{=html}
<div class="section-translation">
  <div class="translation-image">
    <img src="images/dna-1.jpg" alt="DNA Helix">
  </div>
  <div class="translation-content">
    <span class="section-label">ACADEMIC RIGOR</span>
    <h2>Scientific Translation</h2>
    <p>We bridge the gap between visionary discovery and clinical reality by providing rigorous educational frameworks for the world's leading scientists. Our programs are designed to accelerate the transformation of p53 research into tumor-specific therapies that redefine the global standard of oncological care.</p>
    <a href="education.html" class="btn btn-outline-light btn-lg">ExploreCurriculum</a>
  </div>
</div>
```

```{=html}
<script>
document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', function() {
  const translationImage = document.querySelector('.section-translation .translation-image img');
  const translationContainer = document.querySelector('.section-translation');
  
  let animationTriggered = false;
  
  function startUnfurlAnimation() {
    if (animationTriggered) return;
    animationTriggered = true;
    
    let clipPercent = 100;
    const duration = 1500; // 1.5 seconds
    const startTime = Date.now();
    
    function animate() {
      const elapsed = Date.now() - startTime;
      const progress = Math.min(elapsed / duration, 1);
      
      // Easing function for smooth animation
      const easeOut = 1 - Math.pow(1 - progress, 3);
      clipPercent = 100 * (1 - easeOut);
      
      translationImage.style.clipPath = `inset(0 ${clipPercent}% 0 0)`;
      
      if (progress < 1) {
        requestAnimationFrame(animate);
      }
    }
    
    requestAnimationFrame(animate);
  }
  
  window.addEventListener('scroll', function() {
    if (animationTriggered) return;
    
    const rect = translationContainer.getBoundingClientRect();
    const windowHeight = window.innerHeight;
    
    // Trigger when section is 10% into viewport
    if (rect.top <= windowHeight * 0.9 && rect.bottom > 0) {
      startUnfurlAnimation();
    }
  });
});
</script>
```
-->

<!--
```{=html}
<div class="section-network">
  <div class="network-image">
    <img src="images/dna-2.jpg" alt="DNA Research">
  </div>
  <div class="network-content">
    <h2>Pioneering p53 Tumor-Specific Therapeutics</h2>
    <p>Leading the global fight against cancer, Institute 53 focuses on the next generation of therapeutics that selectively target tumor cells through p53 reactivation. Our research translates groundbreaking molecular chemistry into safe, effective clinical potentials, aiming to reduce the global burden of cancer through innovation and precision.</p>
  </div>
</div>
```

```{=html}
<script>
document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', function() {
  const networkImage = document.querySelector('.section-network .network-image img');
  const networkContainer = document.querySelector('.section-network');
  
  let networkAnimationTriggered = false;
  
  function startNetworkUnfurlAnimation() {
    if (networkAnimationTriggered) return;
    networkAnimationTriggered = true;
    
    let clipPercent = 100;
    const duration = 1800; // 1.8 seconds (slightly longer for variety)
    const startTime = Date.now();
    
    function animate() {
      const elapsed = Date.now() - startTime;
      const progress = Math.min(elapsed / duration, 1);
      
      // Easing function for smooth animation
      const easeOut = 1 - Math.pow(1 - progress, 3);
      clipPercent = 100 * (1 - easeOut);
      
      networkImage.style.clipPath = `inset(0 ${clipPercent}% 0 0)`;
      
      if (progress < 1) {
        requestAnimationFrame(animate);
      }
    }
    
    requestAnimationFrame(animate);
  }
  
  window.addEventListener('scroll', function() {
    if (networkAnimationTriggered) return;
    
    const rect = networkContainer.getBoundingClientRect();
    const windowHeight = window.innerHeight;
    
    // Trigger when section is 15% into viewport (slightly different timing)
    if (rect.top <= windowHeight * 0.85 && rect.bottom > 0) {
      startNetworkUnfurlAnimation();
    }
  });
});
</script>
```
-->

```{=html}
<div class="section-join">
  <h2>Join the Network</h2>
  <a href="collaborate.html" class="btn btn-primary btn-lg">Become a Collaborator</a>
</div>
```

::: {.section-pillars}
::: {.container}
[INSTITUTE 53 STRATEGIC PILLARS]{.section-label}

## Transforming Tumor Biology into Targeted Therapies

::: {.pillars-grid}
::: {.pillar-card}
![](images/cell.png)

##### Precision Oncology

Restoring the tumor-suppressive power of p53 to develop therapies that target cancer cells with unmatched genomic precision.
:::

<!-- ::: {.pillar-card}
![](images/neucleus.png)

##### World-Class Education

Providing rigorous academic programs that equip the next generation of global researchers to translate breakthroughs into effective therapies.
::: -->

::: {.pillar-card}
![](images/globe.png)

##### Global Translation

Driving the reach of oncology advancements to healthcare systems worldwide, reducing the global cancer burden through strategic translation.
:::
:::
:::
:::
